refactor(reflect): Expand lock scope to prevent duplicate processing

Critical fix to the reflect command ensuring concurrent processes don't
duplicate work or corrupt state.

## Reflect Command (reflect.ts)

### Lock Scope Expansion
Previously, only the playbook save was locked. This was insufficient because
two concurrent reflect processes could:
1. Both see the same sessions as "unprocessed"
2. Both generate the same deltas
3. Both try to save, with one overwriting the other

Now the entire reflect operation is wrapped in withLock():
- Session discovery
- Diary generation
- Delta validation
- Curation and save
- Processed log update

This ensures atomic reflect operations - only one process handles any
given set of unprocessed sessions.

### Import Fix
Changed `import { withLock } from "../lock.ts"` to `"../lock.js"` for
proper ES module resolution in the compiled output.

## Supporting Changes

### curate.ts
- Minor adjustments for consistency

### playbook.ts
- Minor formatting fixes

### types.ts
- Additional schema refinements discovered during testing

### tsconfig.json
- Configuration adjustments for proper module resolution

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-699","title":"[Validator] generateValidationReport - Detailed validation output","description":"Implement the generateValidationReport function - Create comprehensive validation reports for audit and transparency.

SIGNATURE:
```typescript
function generateValidationReport(
  proposedRule: string,
  gateResult: EvidenceGateResult,
  llmResult?: ValidationResult
): ValidationReport
```

VALIDATION REPORT TYPE:
```typescript
interface ValidationReport {
  proposedRule: string;
  verdict: "ACCEPT" | "REJECT" | "ACCEPT_WITH_CAUTION";
  confidence: number;         // 0.0 to 1.0
  evidence: Array<{
    sessionPath: string;
    agent: string;
    supports: boolean;        // Does this evidence support the rule?
    snippet: string;
  }>;
  gateResult: EvidenceGateResult;
  llmResult?: ValidationResult;
  reason: string;             // Combined reasoning
  refinedRule?: string;       // If LLM suggests improvement
  timestamp: string;
  metadata: {
    sessionCount: number;
    successCount: number;
    failureCount: number;
    llmUsed: boolean;
  };
}
```

REPORT GENERATION LOGIC:
1. Combine gate result and LLM result
2. Determine final verdict:
   - Gate failed → REJECT
   - Gate passed + LLM INVALID → REJECT
   - Gate passed + LLM VALID → ACCEPT
   - Gate passed + LLM VALID_WITH_CAUTION → ACCEPT_WITH_CAUTION
   - Gate passed + no LLM (strong evidence) → ACCEPT
3. Calculate confidence score
4. Format evidence with support indicators
5. Combine reasoning from gate and LLM
6. Include metadata for debugging

CONFIDENCE CALCULATION:
```typescript
if (gateResult.successCount >= 5 && gateResult.failureCount === 0) {
  confidence = 0.95;  // High confidence, no LLM needed
} else if (llmResult) {
  confidence = llmResult.confidence;
} else {
  confidence = 0.5;  // Default for edge cases
}
```

OUTPUT FORMATS:
- JSON: Full structured report
- Markdown: Human-readable summary
- CLI: Colored terminal output with verdict

WHY THIS MATTERS:
- Provides audit trail for all validation decisions
- Makes scientific method transparent
- Helps debug validation logic
- Builds trust in automated rule acceptance

DEPENDENCIES:
- EvidenceGateResult type
- ValidationResult type
- CassHit type

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-69v","title":"[Foundation] package.json with exact dependency versions","description":"Create package.json with exact dependency versions and build configuration for the CASS Memory System CLI tool.

## Context
The CASS Memory System requires a well-defined package.json that supports cross-platform binary compilation, ESM modules, and integration with multiple LLM providers through the Vercel AI SDK.

## Technical Specification

### Dependencies:
- ai@^4.0.0 - Vercel AI SDK core for provider-agnostic LLM integration
- @ai-sdk/openai@^1.0.0 - OpenAI provider (GPT-4, GPT-3.5)
- @ai-sdk/anthropic@^1.0.0 - Anthropic provider (Claude models)
- @ai-sdk/google@^1.0.0 - Google provider (Gemini models)
- zod@^3.23.0 - Runtime type validation for structured LLM outputs
- yaml@^2.3.0 - YAML parsing for playbook and config files
- chalk@^5.3.0 - Terminal output styling for user-friendly CLI

### DevDependencies:
- @types/bun@latest - TypeScript types for Bun runtime
- typescript@^5.0.0 - TypeScript compiler for type checking

### Scripts:
- dev: bun run src/cass-memory.ts - Development mode with hot reload
- build: bun build src/cass-memory.ts --compile --outfile dist/cass-memory - Build for current platform
- build:all: Build for linux, macos, and windows
- build:linux: bun build src/cass-memory.ts --compile --target=linux-x64 --outfile dist/cass-memory-linux
- build:macos: bun build src/cass-memory.ts --compile --target=darwin-arm64 --outfile dist/cass-memory-macos
- build:windows: bun build src/cass-memory.ts --compile --target=windows-x64 --outfile dist/cass-memory-windows.exe
- typecheck: tsc --noEmit - Type checking without compilation
- test: bun test - Run test suite

### Configuration:
- type: module - Enable ES modules
- bin: cass-memory -> ./dist/cass-memory.js - CLI entry point
- name: cass-memory
- version: 0.1.0 - Initial version
- private: true - Not published to npm (distributed as binary)

## Reasoning

### Why Vercel AI SDK:
1. Provider Agnostic: Single interface for OpenAI, Anthropic, Google - users choose their preferred LLM
2. Structured Output: Native Zod integration for reliable structured responses (critical for playbook rule extraction)
3. Streaming Support: Real-time output for long-running operations
4. TypeScript-First: Excellent type safety and IntelliSense
5. Active Development: Well-maintained with regular updates

### Why Bun:
1. Single Binary: Bun can compile TypeScript to standalone executables
2. Fast: Native speed for file operations and process management
3. Built-in TypeScript: No transpilation needed during development
4. Cross-platform: Compile once, run anywhere

### Why ESM:
1. Modern Standard: ES modules are the future of JavaScript
2. Better Tree Shaking: Smaller bundle sizes
3. AI SDK Requirement: Vercel AI SDK v4 requires ESM

## Acceptance Criteria

- package.json exists in project root
- All dependencies listed with correct versions
- All devDependencies listed
- All build scripts present and functional
- type: module is set
- bin entry points to correct file
- bun install works without errors
- bun run dev starts development mode
- bun run build creates executable binary
- bun run typecheck passes with no errors

## How This Serves the Universal Agent Memory System

The package.json is the foundation contract for the entire CASS Memory System. It ensures:

1. Multi-Provider Support: Agents can use any LLM provider their organization prefers
2. Reliable Distribution: Compiled binaries mean no dependency hell for end users
3. Type Safety: Zod validation ensures LLM outputs conform to expected schemas
4. Developer Experience: Clear scripts and tooling enable rapid iteration
5. Cross-Platform: Agents on any OS can use CASS Memory System

Without proper dependency management, the system cannot reliably integrate with LLMs or provide consistent behavior across environments.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:21.842607-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:21.842607-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-6jdf","title":"[Foundation] Define .cass/ repo-level directory structure","description":"Define and implement the .cass/ repository-level directory structure for project-specific CASS Memory System data.

## Context
The .cass/ directory contains project-specific memory that is committed to version control and shared with the entire team. This enables 'memory as code' - the team's collective knowledge about the project is versioned alongside the codebase.

## Technical Specification

### Directory Structure:
.cass/
├── playbook.yaml            # Project-specific playbook (shared with team)
├── config.yaml              # Project-level configuration overrides
└── toxic.log                # Project-level blocked patterns

### File Specifications:

#### playbook.yaml (Project Playbook):
- Project-specific rules that apply to this repository only
- Merged with user's global playbook (~/.cass-memory/playbook.yaml)
- Project rules take precedence over global rules
- Categories: PROJECT_SETUP, BUILD, TEST, DEPLOY, ARCHITECTURE, etc.
- Each bullet has:
  - content: The rule itself
  - context: When/why this rule applies
  - priority: 0-10 (higher = more important)
  - state: PROVEN, TESTING, CANDIDATE
  - created_at: ISO timestamp
  - created_by: User/agent who added it
  - tags: [backend, frontend, ci, etc.]

Example:
yaml
bullets:
  - content: Always run tests with --coverage flag
    context: Team requires 80% coverage for all PRs
    priority: 9
    state: PROVEN
    created_at: 2025-12-01T10:00:00Z
    created_by: cass-memory
    tags: [test, ci]
  
  - content: Use Bun instead of npm for package management
    context: Project uses Bun for faster installs and built-in TypeScript support
    priority: 10
    state: PROVEN
    created_at: 2025-12-01T10:00:00Z
    created_by: cass-memory
    tags: [setup, deps]


#### config.yaml (Project Configuration):
- Overrides for project-specific settings
- Does NOT contain API keys (those stay in ~/.cass-memory/config.json)
- Can specify:
  - preferred_model: Override user's default model for this project
  - enable_curation: Project-level curation toggle
  - evidence_threshold: Custom threshold for this project
  - excluded_paths: Paths to ignore in reflections (e.g., generated/, vendor/)
  - custom_categories: Project-specific bullet categories

Example:
yaml
preferred_model: gpt-4  # This project uses GPT-4 for complex reasoning
enable_curation: true
evidence_threshold: 5
excluded_paths:
  - generated/
  - vendor/
  - node_modules/
custom_categories:
  - ARCHITECTURE
  - API_DESIGN
  - DATABASE


#### toxic.log (Project Blocklist):
- Newline-delimited list of blocked bullet content
- Project-specific patterns to block
- Committed to git so entire team blocks same patterns
- Case-insensitive matching
- Merged with user's global toxic_bullets.log

Example:
text
Always use var for variables
Disable all linting rules
Skip writing tests for this file


## Reasoning

### Why .cass/ (Hidden Directory):
1. Unix Convention: Hidden directories for metadata
2. Doesn't Clutter: Not visible in normal directory listings
3. Tool Convention: Like .git/, .github/, .vscode/

### Why playbook.yaml in Git:
1. Memory as Code: Team knowledge is versioned
2. Shared Learning: New team members inherit project wisdom
3. Code Review: Changes to playbook go through PR review
4. Reproducibility: Same rules apply to all developers
5. Time Travel: git log shows how project knowledge evolved

### Why config.yaml Separate from config.json:
1. Security: config.json (global) has API keys, never committed
2. Team Settings: config.yaml (repo) has project settings, always committed
3. Overrides: Repo settings override user settings

### Why toxic.log in Git:
1. Team Safety: Everyone blocks the same bad patterns
2. Onboarding: New devs see what NOT to do
3. Historical Record: Track why certain patterns were blocked
4. Consistency: Entire team learns from mistakes

### Why Project Playbook Takes Precedence:
1. Specificity: Project needs override general preferences
2. Team Alignment: Everyone follows project standards
3. Flexibility: Users keep their personal rules for other projects

## Acceptance Criteria

- .cass/ directory is created on cass-memory init (repo mode)
- playbook.yaml created with empty bullets array
- config.yaml created with sensible defaults (or omitted if no overrides)
- toxic.log created as empty file
- .cass/ directory is NOT in .gitignore (should be committed)
- Helper functions in utils.ts: resolveRepoDir(), ensureRepoStructure()
- Playbook loading merges global + repo playbooks (repo takes precedence)
- Config loading merges global + repo configs (repo takes precedence)
- Toxic patterns merged from both global and repo logs
- cass-memory doctor verifies .cass/ structure

## How This Serves the Universal Agent Memory System

The .cass/ directory enables team-level memory sharing:

1. Memory as Code: Team knowledge is versioned alongside code
2. Collective Learning: Entire team benefits from lessons learned
3. Onboarding: New team members inherit project wisdom immediately
4. Consistency: All agents follow same project-specific rules
5. Code Review: Memory changes go through same review process as code
6. Time Travel: git history shows evolution of team knowledge
7. Multi-Agent Coordination: Multiple agents share same memory layer

This is the key innovation that makes CASS Memory a universal system - memory persists not just per user, but per project, and is versioned in git like any other critical project artifact.

Without repo-level playbooks, teams would constantly reinvent the wheel, onboarding would be slow, and agents would have inconsistent behavior across team members.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:47:57.98691-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:47:57.98691-05:00"}
-{"id":"cass_memory_system-6ob","title":"[Playbook] findSimilarBullet - Find most similar existing bullet","description":"## Function Signature
```typescript
function findSimilarBullet(
  bullets: PlaybookBullet[],
  content: string,
  threshold: number
): { found: boolean; bullet?: PlaybookBullet; similarity?: number }
```

## Purpose
Find the most similar existing bullet above a threshold. Returns similarity score for logging and decision-making.

## Algorithm
1. Filter out deprecated bullets
2. Calculate similarity for ALL non-deprecated bullets
3. Find bullet with highest similarity
4. If highest >= threshold:
   - Return { found: true, bullet, similarity }
5. Else:
   - Return { found: false }

## Difference from isDuplicateBullet
- **isDuplicateBullet**: Returns first match, boolean check, prevents duplicates
- **findSimilarBullet**: Returns highest match, includes score, suggests similar

## Return Value Usage
```typescript
const result = findSimilarBullet(bullets, newContent, 0.7);
if (result.found) {
  console.log(`Found similar bullet (${result.similarity.toFixed(2)}): ${result.bullet.content}`);
  // Ask user: merge, replace, or add anyway?
}
```

## Threshold Selection
- **High threshold (0.85+)**: Only very similar bullets
- **Medium threshold (0.70-0.85)**: Suggest as related
- **Low threshold (0.50-0.70)**: Broad similarity search

## Implementation Notes
- Must search ALL bullets to find highest
- Skip deprecated bullets (outdated)
- Include similarity score in result
- Useful for merge suggestions

## Usage Scenarios
1. **Interactive add**: Show similar bullets to user
2. **Merge suggestions**: Find candidates for combining
3. **Search**: Find bullets related to query
4. **Analytics**: Measure playbook clustering

## Dependencies
- jaccardSimilarity() utility function

## Related Functions
- isDuplicateBullet() - simpler boolean check
- findMergeCandidates() - finds multiple similar groups
- getBulletsByCategory() - category-based filtering","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:00.773781-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:47:39.936514-05:00"}

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-6u3","title":"[Utils] truncateForContext - Large content truncation for LLM","description":"","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:16.512693-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:16.512693-05:00","comments":[{"id":4,"issue_id":"cass_memory_system-6u3","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Function Signature
```typescript
function truncateForContext(text: string, maxChars: number): string
```

## Implementation Details
- Check if text.length <= maxChars
- If yes, return text unchanged
- If no, truncate and append informative message:
  - text.slice(0, maxChars) + "\n\n[... truncated for context limit ...]"
- Use \n\n for clear separation
- Message explains WHY truncated (not just "...")

## Why This Matters
Used before sending session content to LLM to avoid token limits. Typical maxChars might be 100,000 (roughly 25k tokens). Clear message helps LLM understand context was truncated.

## Edge Cases to Handle
- Very small maxChars - ensure message fits or adjust
- Empty string - return empty
- Exactly at limit - return unchanged
- Multiple sessions concatenated - truncate whole string, not per session
- Preserve important content at start (don't truncate from middle)","created_at":"2025-12-07T23:15:50Z"}]}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-72k","title":"[Validator] runLLMValidation - Scientific validation via LLM","description":"Implement the runLLMValidation function - Scientific validation via LLM using historical evidence.

SIGNATURE:
```typescript
async function runLLMValidation(
  proposedRule: string,
  history: CassHit[],
  config: Config
): Promise<ValidationResult>
```

VALIDATION RESULT:
```typescript
interface ValidationResult {
  verdict: "VALID" | "INVALID" | "VALID_WITH_CAUTION";
  confidence: number;          // 0.0 to 1.0
  reason: string;              // LLM explanation
  evidence: string[];          // Citations from history
  refinedRule?: string;        // Optional improved wording
}
```

USES PROMPTS.validator TEMPLATE:
```
You are a scientific validator for agent playbook rules.

PROPOSED RULE:
{proposedRule}

HISTORICAL EVIDENCE:
{formattedEvidence}

TASK:
Analyze whether this rule is supported by the historical evidence.
Consider:
- Does the evidence show this pattern worked?
- Are there counter-examples (failures)?
- Is the rule too generic or too specific?
- Is the wording actionable?

RESPOND WITH:
- VALID: Strong evidence supports this rule
- INVALID: Evidence contradicts or doesn't support this rule
- VALID_WITH_CAUTION: Some support but concerns exist

Include:
1. Your verdict
2. Confidence score (0.0-1.0)
3. Reasoning based on evidence
4. Optional refined wording if the concept is valid but phrasing needs work
```

VALIDATION CRITERIA:
- Pattern appears in successful sessions
- Few or no counter-examples in failed sessions
- Rule is specific and actionable
- Rule is not overly generic
- Wording is clear and unambiguous

WHY THIS MATTERS:
- Provides deep analysis beyond simple counting
- Can identify nuanced patterns humans might miss
- Suggests improvements to rule wording
- Adds scientific rigor to rule acceptance

DEPENDENCIES:
- formatEvidenceForLLM() - Format cass hits for prompt
- PROMPTS.validator - Validation prompt template
- LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)
- CassHit type

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-e8x","title":"[Playbook] loadPlaybook - Load and validate playbook from YAML","description":"## Function Signature
```typescript
async function loadPlaybook(config: Config): Promise<Playbook>
```

## Purpose
Load and validate playbook from YAML file. Handles missing files, corruption, and schema validation with automatic recovery.

## Algorithm
1. Expand playbookPath from config
2. Check if file exists
3. If not exists: create empty playbook and return
4. Read file content
5. If empty: create new playbook and return
6. Parse YAML content
7. Normalize keys (snake_case → camelCase for compatibility)
8. Validate with PlaybookSchema.safeParse()
9. If invalid: backup to .backup.{timestamp} and create new
10. Return validated playbook

## Error Handling
- **ENOENT**: File doesn't exist → create empty
- **EACCES**: Permission denied → throw error
- **Parse errors**: YAML syntax errors → backup and recreate
- **Validation errors**: Schema mismatch → backup and recreate

## Backup Strategy
When corruption detected:
- Backup to: `{playbookPath}.backup.{timestamp}`
- Log warning with backup location
- Create fresh empty playbook
- Never lose user data

## Implementation Notes
- Use fs.promises for async file operations
- Use yaml.parse() for YAML parsing
- Normalize keys for backward compatibility
- Always validate against current schema version

## Dependencies
- fs/promises
- yaml library
- PlaybookSchema (Zod schema)
- createEmptyPlaybook()

## Related Functions
- createEmptyPlaybook() - creates new structure
- savePlaybook() - writes loaded playbook back","notes":"Existing playbook helper implementations already present; no code changes made. -{"id":"cass_memory_system-efd","title":"[Types] PlaybookBullet Schema Part 1 - Identity and Scope","description":"## Overview
Define identity and scope fields for PlaybookBullet schema. These fields establish bullet uniqueness and applicability hierarchy.

## Identity Field

### id: string
- Format: 'b-{timestamp36}-{random}'
- Example: 'b-l7k9m2p4-a3f8'
- Generation: timestamp in base36 + 4-char random suffix
- Purpose: Globally unique, sortable identifier
- Immutable: Never changes after creation

## Scope Hierarchy (5 Levels)

### scope: enum
Type: 'global' | 'workspace' | 'language' | 'framework' | 'task'

Hierarchy (broadest to narrowest):

1. global - Universal rules (e.g., 'Write clear commit messages')
   - Applies: Always
   - scopeKey: Not used
   - workspace: Not used

2. workspace - Repository-specific rules
   - Applies: When in matching workspace directory
   - scopeKey: Not used (workspace field holds path)
   - workspace: '/path/to/project'
   - Example: 'Use AuthService from @/lib/auth in this project'

3. language - Language-specific rules
   - Applies: When working in files of that language
   - scopeKey: Language identifier (e.g., 'typescript', 'python', 'rust')
   - workspace: Optional (can be workspace + language)
   - Example: 'For TypeScript, prefer interfaces over types for objects'

4. framework - Framework/library-specific rules
   - Applies: When project uses that framework
   - scopeKey: Framework identifier (e.g., 'nextjs', 'react', 'django')
   - workspace: Optional
   - Example: 'For Next.js, use app router not pages router'

5. task - Task-type specific rules

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-ehaq","title":"[CLI:stats] computeFullStats - Calculate all metrics","description":"Implements the computeFullStats function that calculates comprehensive playbook metrics.

FUNCTION SIGNATURE:
```typescript
interface PlaybookStats {
  total: number;
  byScope: { global: number; workspace: number };
  byMaturity: { 
    candidate: number; 
    established: number; 
    proven: number; 
    deprecated: number 
  };
  byType: { rule: number; antiPattern: number };
  scoreDistribution: { 
    excellent: number;    // score >= 8
    good: number;         // score 4-7
    neutral: number;      // score 0-3
    atRisk: number;       // score < 0
  };
  topPerformers: { 
    id: string; 
    score: number; 
    preview: string 
  }[];
  atRisk: { 
    id: string; 
    score: number; 
    reason: string 
  }[];
  stale: { 
    id: string; 
    daysSinceLastFeedback: number 
  }[];
  mergeCandidates: { 
    ids: string[];        // Array of 2+ similar bullet IDs
    similarity: number;   // Semantic similarity score
  }[];
}

function computeFullStats(playbook: Playbook): PlaybookStats
```

COMPUTATION LOGIC:

1. BASIC COUNTS:
   - Iterate all bullets, count by scope/maturity/type
   - Skip deprecated unless explicitly included

2. SCORE DISTRIBUTION:
   - Calculate effective score (with decay) for each bullet
   - Bucket into excellent/good/neutral/atRisk
   - Use consistent thresholds across system

3. TOP PERFORMERS:
   - Sort by effective score descending
   - Take top 10
   - Generate preview (first 60 chars of content)

4. AT RISK BULLETS:
   - Filter bullets with score < 0
   - Include reason: "Negative feedback" or "Declining effectiveness"
   - Sort by score ascending (worst first)

5. STALE BULLETS:
   - Calculate days since last helpful/harmful feedback
   - Filter bullets with >90 days (configurable threshold)
   - Include only active bullets (not deprecated)

6. MERGE CANDIDATES:
   - Compute pairwise semantic similarity (if embeddings available)
   - Find pairs/groups with similarity > 0.90
   - Exclude already-merged or deprecated bullets
   - Limit to top 5 groups

PERFORMANCE OPTIMIZATIONS:
- Cache embeddings for similarity computation
- Use single pass for multiple aggregations
- Lazy compute merge candidates (expensive)
- Parallel score calculations for large playbooks

EDGE CASES:
- Empty playbook → return zeros
- No embeddings → skip merge candidates
- All bullets deprecated → show warning
- No feedback data → mark all as stale

DEPENDENCIES:
- Playbook data structure
- Score calculation utilities
- Embedding similarity (optional)
- Date/time utilities for staleness

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- All metrics accurate and consistent
- Performance <500ms for 1000 bullets
- Handles edge cases gracefully
- Stats match reality when spot-checked","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:53:34.560049-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:53:34.560049-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-ekh","title":"[Types] Validation Types - Scientific Validation Results","description":"## Overview
Define types for scientific validation system - results from evidence-based rule validation before adding to playbook.

## Purpose
Scientific validation prevents low-quality rules from entering the playbook by:
1. Searching historical sessions for supporting evidence
2. Analyzing success/failure patterns
3. Providing confidence scores
4. Suggesting state (active vs draft)
5. Optionally refining rule text

## ValidationResult (Simple)

Basic validation outcome for quick checks.

Fields:
- valid: boolean (pass or fail)
- reason: string (explanation of verdict)
- refinedContent: string | undefined (optional improved rule text)
- confidence: number | undefined (0-1 confidence score)

Usage: Quick validation checks, preliminary filtering

Example:
{ valid: true, reason: 'Found 5 supporting sessions with consistent pattern', confidence: 0.87 }

{ valid: false, reason: 'No evidence found in recent sessions', confidence: 0.12 }

## ValidationReport (Comprehensive)

Detailed validation analysis with evidence and recommendations.

Fields:

proposedRule: string
- The rule being validated
- Example: 'Use TokenValidator for JWT validation'

verdict: 'ACCEPT' | 'REJECT' | 'ACCEPT_WITH_CAUTION'
- ACCEPT: Strong evidence, add to playbook as active
- REJECT: Insufficient/contradictory evidence, do not add
- ACCEPT_WITH_CAUTION: Some evidence, add as draft or candidate

confidence: number
- Range: 0-1
- Based on: evidence quality, quantity, recency
- Example: 0.85 (high confidence)

evidence: Evidence[]
- Array of supporting/contradicting evidence
- Structure: { sessionPath: string, snippet: string, type: 'supporting' | 'contradicting', relevance: number }
- Example: [{ sessionPath: '/cass/2025-12-07.jsonl', snippet: 'Used TokenValidator successfully', type: 'supporting', relevance: 0.92 }]

reason: string
- Detailed explanation of verdict
- Includes: evidence summary, patterns observed, concerns
- Example: 'Found 8 supporting sessions over 30 days showing consistent use of TokenValidator for JWT validation. No contradicting patterns. Recommend adding as active rule.'

refinedRule: string | undefined
- Optional improved version of proposed rule
- Enhanced with: specificity, context, examples
- Example: 'Use TokenValidator from @/lib/auth for all JWT validation to prevent authentication bypass'

## Evidence Structure

Supporting evidence for validation.

Fields:
- sessionPath: string (path to session file)
- snippet: string (relevant excerpt from session)
- type: 'supporting' | 'contradicting'
- relevance: number (0-1, how relevant this evidence is)
- timestamp: string | undefined (when session occurred)
- agent: string | undefined (which agent)

## EvidenceGateResult

Result from pre-LLM evidence gate check.

Purpose: Fast filtering before expensive LLM validation

Fields:

passed: boolean
- True if enough evidence to proceed to LLM validation
- False if insufficient evidence (skip LLM, save cost)

reason: string
- Explanation of gate decision
- Example: 'Found 8 sessions with 6 successes, 1 failure - sufficient for validation'

suggestedState: 'draft' | 'active'
- Recommended initial state based on evidence
- draft: Low evidence (1-2 sessions)
- active: Strong evidence (5+ sessions)

sessionCount: number
- Total sessions found matching proposed rule
- Example: 8

successCount: number
- Sessions with positive outcomes
- Example: 6

failureCount: number
- Sessions with negative outcomes
- Example: 1

Example:
{ passed: true, reason: '8 sessions found (6 success, 1 failure, 1 mixed)', suggestedState: 'active', sessionCount: 8, successCount: 6, failureCount: 1 }

{ passed: false, reason: 'Only 1 session found - insufficient evidence', suggestedState: 'draft', sessionCount: 1, successCount: 1, failureCount: 0 }

## Validation Workflow

1. Evidence Gate: Run fast pre-check (EvidenceGateResult)
2. Gate Decision:
   - If passed: Proceed to LLM validation
   - If not passed: Either reject or add as draft (configurable)
3. LLM Validation: Detailed analysis (ValidationReport)
4. Apply Result:
   - ACCEPT: Add to playbook as active (or established if strong evidence)
   - ACCEPT_WITH_CAUTION: Add as draft or candidate
   - REJECT: Don't add, optionally log for future

## Configuration

validationEnabled: boolean (config, default true)
validationLookbackDays: number (config, default 90)
Evidence gate thresholds (configurable):
- minSessionsForActive: 5
- minSessionsForDraft: 2
- maxFailureRatio: 0.3 (30% failures still acceptable)

## Acceptance Criteria
- ValidationResult interface with all fields
- ValidationReport interface with comprehensive structure
- Evidence interface for evidence items
- EvidenceGateResult interface for pre-LLM gate
- Zod schemas for all types
- TypeScript exports
- Example validation reports showing ACCEPT/REJECT/CAUTION
- JSDoc documentation
- Integration with validation command","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:06.53211-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:58:26.623953-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-eoba","title":"[CLI:doctor] checkCassIntegration - Verify cass setup","description":"Implements the checkCassIntegration function that verifies cass CLI setup and health.

FUNCTION SIGNATURE:
```typescript
interface HealthCheck {
  category: string;
  item: string;
  status: 'pass' | 'warn' | 'fail';
  message: string;
  fixable?: boolean;
  fix?: () => Promise<void>;
}

async function checkCassIntegration(cassPath?: string): Promise<HealthCheck[]>
```

CHECKS PERFORMED:

1. CASS BINARY:
   - Check if cass exists in PATH
   - Verify executable permissions
   - Get version: `cass --version`
   - Validate minimum version (if required)
   - Status: FAIL if not found, PASS otherwise

2. VERSION COMPATIBILITY:
   - Parse cass version string
   - Check against minimum required version
   - Warn if version is very old/unsupported
   - Status: WARN if old but functional, FAIL if incompatible

3. INDEX HEALTH:
   - Run: `cass index-info` (or equivalent)
   - Check last update timestamp
   - Warn if index >7 days stale
   - Check for corruption markers
   - Status: WARN if stale, FAIL if corrupted

4. SESSION COUNT:
   - Query total session count
   - Report storage size
   - Warn if very large (>5GB)
   - Status: PASS or WARN (info only)

5. AGENT BREAKDOWN:
   - Analyze sessions by agent type
   - Report usage distribution
   - Identify most-used agents/tools
   - Status: PASS (info only)

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:

```typescript
// Check 1: Binary exists
const cassExists = await which('cass');
if (!cassExists) {
  return [{
    category: 'Cass Integration',
    item: 'Binary',
    status: 'fail',
    message: 'cass not found in PATH. Install from https://github.com/...',
    fixable: false
  }];
}

// Check 2: Version
const { stdout } = await exec('cass --version');
const version = parseVersion(stdout);
if (version < MIN_VERSION) {
  return [{
    category: 'Cass Integration',
    item: 'Version',
    status: 'fail',
    message: `cass version ${version} is too old. Minimum: ${MIN_VERSION}`,
    fixable: false
  }];
}

// Check 3: Index health
const indexInfo = await exec('cass index-info --json');
const data = JSON.parse(indexInfo.stdout);
const daysSinceUpdate = dateDiff(data.lastUpdate, now());
if (daysSinceUpdate > 7) {
  return [{
    category: 'Cass Integration',
    item: 'Index Freshness',
    status: 'warn',
    message: `Index last updated ${daysSinceUpdate} days ago`,
    fixable: true,
    fix: async () => exec('cass rebuild-index')
  }];
}
```

ERROR HANDLING:
- Gracefully handle missing cass binary
- Parse errors → treat as WARN (cass might be old version)
- Timeout on slow commands (5s max)
- Sanitize error messages (hide sensitive paths if needed)

DEPENDENCIES:
- which (find binary in PATH)
- child_process.exec (run cass commands)
- Version parsing utilities
- Date/time utilities

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Detects cass presence accurately
- Version parsing works for all cass versions
- Index health check is reliable
- Performance: all checks complete in <2 seconds
- Clear actionable messages for failures","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:54:49.813974-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:54:49.813974-05:00"}

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-ngd","title":"[Security] Pattern breadth testing in doctor command","description":"Implement pattern breadth testing in the doctor command to detect over-aggressive patterns.

Test purpose:
- Detect patterns that are too broad (match benign text)
- Warn about patterns with excessive false positives
- Suggest more specific alternatives

Implementation in doctor command:
```typescript
function testPatternBreadth(patterns: typeof SECRET_PATTERNS) {
  const benignSamples = [
    "The tokenizer splits text into tokens",
    "Bearer of bad news",
    "This is a password-protected file",
    "The API key concept is important"
  ];

  for (const { pattern, replacement } of patterns) {
    for (const sample of benignSamples) {
      if (pattern.test(sample)) {
        console.warn(`[doctor] Pattern too broad: ${pattern}`);
        console.warn(`  Matches benign text: "${sample}"`);
        console.warn(`  Consider more specific pattern`);
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Warning: Pattern too broad: /token.*/gi

  Consider more specific pattern like /token["\s:=]+/

-{"id":"cass_memory_system-np4","title":"[Types] PlaybookBullet Schema Part 2 - Content, Type, and Kind","description":"## Overview
Define content, classification, and semantic categorization fields for PlaybookBullet. These fields determine WHAT the rule says and HOW it should be presented.

## Content Fields

### category: string
- Purpose: High-level grouping for organization
- Type: String (not strict enum - allows flexibility)
- Common values: 'testing', 'git', 'debugging', 'api-design', 'security', 'performance', 'documentation'
- Usage: Grouping in playbook display, filtering in search
- Example: 'testing' for all test-related rules

### content: string
- Purpose: The actual rule text shown to agents
- Length: 10-500 characters (enforced by validation)
- Format: Clear, imperative statement
- Examples: 'Use vitest for unit tests, not jest' / 'Validate JWT tokens before trusting claims' / 'Run pnpm test before committing'
- Style: Action-oriented, specific, actionable

### searchPointer: string | undefined
- Purpose: Gemini-style pattern for retrieving detailed examples
- Format: cass query pattern (e.g., 'auth* AND token validation')
- Usage: When rule needs deep context, agent can run 'cass search <searchPointer>'
- Example: 'JWT validation patterns' -> searches cass for relevant examples
- Benefit: Keep bullets concise while providing depth on demand

## Type Classification (Binary)

### type: 'rule' | 'anti-pattern'
- rule: Prescriptive guidance (do this)
- anti-pattern: Proscriptive warning (don't do this)

### isNegative: boolean
- Purpose: Computed shorthand for type === 'anti-pattern'
- Usage: Quick filtering and display logic
- Derivation: Automatically set based on type field

Display Impact:
- Rules: Shown in main playbook sections
- Anti-patterns: Shown in separate 'PITFALLS' section

## Semantic Kind (4 Categories)

### kind: 'project_convention' | 'stack_pattern' | 'workflow_rule' | 'anti_pattern'

#### project_convention
- Definition: Repository-specific conventions
- Portability: LOW - unique to this codebase
- Scope: Usually workspace-scoped
- Examples: 'Use AuthService from @/lib/auth for authentication' / 'Store config in config/app.yaml, not .env' / 'Use JobQueue class for background tasks'
- Characteristics: References specific files/modules/patterns in THIS project

#### stack_pattern
- Definition: Reusable patterns for languages/frameworks
- Portability: HIGH - transferable across projects
- Scope: Usually language or framework-scoped
- Examples: 'For React hooks, test effects with renderHook from @testing-library/react-hooks' / 'In TypeScript, use const assertions for literal arrays' / 'For Next.js, prefer server components over client components'
- Characteristics: Generic best practices for the tech stack

#### workflow_rule
- Definition: Process and methodology rules
- Portability: MEDIUM - often org-specific
- Scope: Usually global or workspace
- Examples: 'Run pnpm test before committing' / 'Write commit messages in conventional commits format' / 'Update changelog in CHANGELOG.md for user-facing changes'
- Characteristics: About HOW to work, not WHAT to code

#### anti_pattern
- Definition: Pitfalls and mistakes to avoid
- Portability: VARIABLE
- Scope: Any scope
- Examples: 'Don't mock Router hooks directly - use mockRouter utility' / 'Avoid useState for derived values - use useMemo' / 'Don't commit .env files with secrets'
- Characteristics: Negative guidance, often learned from past mistakes
- Display: Shown in PITFALLS section, not main playbook

## Kind vs Type vs Scope

Field | Purpose | Values | Controls
scope | WHERE it applies | global/workspace/language/framework/task | Filtering by context
type | DO or DON'T | rule/anti-pattern | Display section
kind | WHY it exists | project_convention/stack_pattern/workflow_rule/anti_pattern | Portability/semantics

## Examples

Project Convention Rule: { category: 'authentication', content: 'Use TokenValidator from @/lib/auth for all JWT validation', searchPointer: 'TokenValidator usage examples', type: 'rule', isNegative: false, kind: 'project_convention' }

Stack Pattern Rule: { category: 'testing', content: 'For React hooks, use renderHook from @testing-library/react', type: 'rule', isNegative: false, kind: 'stack_pattern' }

Workflow Rule: { category: 'git', content: 'Run full test suite before pushing to main', type: 'rule', isNegative: false, kind: 'workflow_rule' }

Anti-Pattern: { category: 'testing', content: 'Don't mock useRouter - use mockRouter from test/utils', type: 'anti-pattern', isNegative: true, kind: 'anti_pattern' }

## Acceptance Criteria
- category field as flexible string
- content field with 10-500 char validation
- searchPointer optional field
- type enum with 2 values
- isNegative computed boolean
- kind enum with 4 values
- Examples for all kind types
- JSDoc explaining kind semantics
- Content length validation in schema","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:44:58.784179-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:51:45.593958-05:00"}

## Content Fields

### category: string
- Purpose: High-level grouping for organization
- Type: String (not strict enum - allows flexibility)
- Common values: 'testing', 'git', 'debugging', 'api-design', 'security', 'performance', 'documentation'
- Usage: Grouping in playbook display, filtering in search
- Example: 'testing' for all test-related rules

### content: string
- Purpose: The actual rule text shown to agents
- Length: 10-500 characters (enforced by validation)
- Format: Clear, imperative statement
- Examples: 'Use vitest for unit tests, not jest' / 'Validate JWT tokens before trusting claims' / 'Run pnpm test before committing'
- Style: Action-oriented, specific, actionable

### searchPointer: string | undefined
- Purpose: Gemini-style pattern for retrieving detailed examples
- Format: cass query pattern (e.g., 'auth* AND token validation')
- Usage: When rule needs deep context, agent can run 'cass search <searchPointer>'
- Example: 'JWT validation patterns' -> searches cass for relevant examples
- Benefit: Keep bullets concise while providing depth on demand

## Type Classification (Binary)

### type: 'rule' | 'anti-pattern'
- rule: Prescriptive guidance (do this)
- anti-pattern: Proscriptive warning (don't do this)

### isNegative: boolean
- Purpose: Computed shorthand for type === 'anti-pattern'
- Usage: Quick filtering and display logic
- Derivation: Automatically set based on type field

Display Impact:
- Rules: Shown in main playbook sections
- Anti-patterns: Shown in separate 'PITFALLS' section

## Semantic Kind (4 Categories)

### kind: 'project_convention' | 'stack_pattern' | 'workflow_rule' | 'anti_pattern'

#### project_convention
- Definition: Repository-specific conventions
- Portability: LOW - unique to this codebase
- Scope: Usually workspace-scoped
- Examples: 'Use AuthService from @/lib/auth for authentication' / 'Store config in config/app.yaml, not .env' / 'Use JobQueue class for background tasks'
- Characteristics: References specific files/modules/patterns in THIS project

#### stack_pattern
- Definition: Reusable patterns for languages/frameworks
- Portability: HIGH - transferable across projects
- Scope: Usually language or framework-scoped
- Examples: 'For React hooks, test effects with renderHook from @testing-library/react-hooks' / 'In TypeScript, use const assertions for literal arrays' / 'For Next.js, prefer server components over client components'
- Characteristics: Generic best practices for the tech stack

#### workflow_rule
- Definition: Process and methodology rules
- Portability: MEDIUM - often org-specific
- Scope: Usually global or workspace
- Examples: 'Run pnpm test before committing' / 'Write commit messages in conventional commits format' / 'Update changelog in CHANGELOG.md for user-facing changes'
- Characteristics: About HOW to work, not WHAT to code

#### anti_pattern
- Definition: Pitfalls and mistakes to avoid
- Portability: VARIABLE
- Scope: Any scope
- Examples: 'Don't mock Router hooks directly - use mockRouter utility' / 'Avoid useState for derived values - use useMemo' / 'Don't commit .env files with secrets'
- Characteristics: Negative guidance, often learned from past mistakes
- Display: Shown in PITFALLS section, not main playbook

## Kind vs Type vs Scope

Field | Purpose | Values | Controls
scope | WHERE it applies | global/workspace/language/framework/task | Filtering by context
type | DO or DON'T | rule/anti-pattern | Display section
kind | WHY it exists | project_convention/stack_pattern/workflow_rule/anti_pattern | Portability/semantics

## Examples

Project Convention Rule: { category: 'authentication', content: 'Use TokenValidator from @/lib/auth for all JWT validation', searchPointer: 'TokenValidator usage examples', type: 'rule', isNegative: false, kind: 'project_convention' }

Stack Pattern Rule: { category: 'testing', content: 'For React hooks, use renderHook from @testing-library/react', type: 'rule', isNegative: false, kind: 'stack_pattern' }

Workflow Rule: { category: 'git', content: 'Run full test suite before pushing to main', type: 'rule', isNegative: false, kind: 'workflow_rule' }

Anti-Pattern: { category: 'testing', content: 'Don't mock useRouter - use mockRouter from test/utils', type: 'anti-pattern', isNegative: true, kind: 'anti_pattern' }

## Acceptance Criteria
- category field as flexible string
- content field with 10-500 char validation
- searchPointer optional field
- type enum with 2 values
- isNegative computed boolean
- kind enum with 4 values
- Examples for all kind types
- JSDoc explaining kind semantics
- Content length validation in schema","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:44:58.784179-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T20:01:12.826505-05:00"}

## Purpose
Add intelligent features that dramatically improve relevance and usability:
local semantic search, multi-iteration reflection, scientific validation,
and playbook health analytics.

## Background & Reasoning
Simple keyword matching for bullet relevance achieves ~60% accuracy. By adding
local semantic embeddings via @xenova/transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 23MB model),
we can reach ~90% relevance without network calls.

The GPT Pro proposal introduced "scientific validation" - before accepting a new
rule, query cass for historical evidence. Did this pattern succeed or fail in the
past? This prevents accepting rules that contradict historical evidence.

Multi-iteration reflection (from ACE paper) runs the reflector multiple times per
session, focusing on different aspects each iteration. This catches insights that
a single pass might miss.

## Priority Features (from ROI analysis)
- P7: Local semantic search @xenova/transformers (4× impact)
- P9: Stats command playbook health dashboard (2× impact)

## Key Deliverables
1. `cass-memory validate "<rule>"` - Scientific validation against history
2. `cass-memory stats` - Playbook health dashboard
3. Local embedding-based relevance scoring
4. Multi-iteration reflector (configurable 1-3 iterations)
5. Cross-agent enrichment in diaries

## Technical Specifications
### Semantic Search
- Model: Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (runs locally, ~23MB)
- Embedding dimension: 384
- Scoring: 40% keyword + 60% semantic similarity
- Cache: ~/.cass-memory/embeddings/bullets.embeddings

### Stats Dashboard Metrics
- Total bullets by scope (global/workspace)
- Distribution by maturity (candidate/established/proven/retired)
- Distribution by kind (project_convention/stack_pattern/workflow_rule/anti_pattern)
- Top performers (highest effective scores)
- At-risk bullets (negative effective scores)
- Stale bullets (no feedback in 90+ days)
- Merge candidates (high semantic similarity pairs)

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-y8o.1","title":"Validate Command: Scientific Validation (GPT Pro Pattern)","description":"# Feature: Validate Command (Scientific Validation)

## Purpose
Validate a proposed rule against historical evidence from cass.
This implements the GPT Pro proposal's "scientific validation" - 
treating rule proposals as hypotheses to be tested against data.

## Usage
```bash
cass-memory validate "Always use httpx instead of requests" --json
cass-memory validate "Prefer composition over inheritance" --verbose
```

## Process Flow
```
1. Extract validation keywords from proposed rule
2. Check evidence-count gate (auto-accept/reject if clear signal)
3. If ambiguous, run LLM validator with evidence
4. Return verdict with reasoning
```

## Implementation
```typescript
async function validateCommand(
  proposedRule: string,
  flags: ValidateFlags
): Promise<void> {
  const config = await loadConfig();
  
  // 1. Extract keywords
  const keywords = extractKeywords(proposedRule);
  
  // 2. Check evidence gate
  const gateResult = await evidenceCountGate(proposedRule, config);
  
  if (gateResult.action !== "needs-llm") {
    output({
      proposedRule,
      verdict: gateResult.action === "auto-accept" ? "ACCEPT" : "REJECT",
      confidence: gateResult.confidence,
      reason: `${gateResult.action}: ${gateResult.successCount} successes, ${gateResult.failureCount} failures`,
      evidence: gateResult.relevantSessions.map(h => ({
        session: h.source_path,
        snippet: h.snippet,
        outcome: classifySessionOutcome(h)
      }))
    }, formatValidationResult, flags);
    return;
  }
  
  // 3. Run LLM validator
  const validation = await runValidator(
    ValidationResultSchema,
    proposedRule,
    formatEvidenceForPrompt(gateResult.relevantSessions),
    config
  );
  
  output({
    proposedRule,
    verdict: validation.valid ? "ACCEPT" : "REJECT",
    confidence: validation.confidence || 0.7,
    reason: validation.reason,
    refinedRule: validation.refinedContent,
    evidence: gateResult.relevantSessions.map(h => ({
      session: h.source_path,
      snippet: h.snippet
    }))
  }, formatValidationResult, flags);
}
```

  "verdict": "ACCEPT_WITH_CAUTION",

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-y8o.3","title":"Local Semantic Search: @xenova/transformers (P7)","description":"# Feature: Local Semantic Search (P7 - 4× Relevance Impact)

## Purpose
Dramatically improve bullet relevance matching by using local semantic
embeddings. Catches synonyms and conceptual matches that keyword search misses.

## The Problem
Keyword matching misses:
- "authentication" vs "auth" vs "login" vs "session"
- "error handling" vs "exception management" vs "failure recovery"
- "performance optimization" vs "speed improvement" vs "making it faster"

## Solution: Local Embeddings
Use @xenova/transformers with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model:
- Runs 100% locally (no network calls)
- ~23MB model download (one time)
- ~3ms per embedding on modern laptop
- 384-dimension dense vectors

## Implementation
```typescript
import { pipeline } from "@xenova/transformers";

let embedder: any = null;

async function getEmbedder() {
  if (!embedder) {
    embedder = await pipeline(
      "feature-extraction",
      "Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2"
    );
  }
  return embedder;
}

async function embedText(text: string): Promise<number[]> {
  const embed = await getEmbedder();
  const result = await embed(text, { pooling: "mean", normalize: true });
  return Array.from(result.data);
}

function cosineSimilarity(a: number[], b: number[]): number {
  let dot = 0, normA = 0, normB = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < a.length; i++) {
    dot += a[i] * b[i];
    normA += a[i] * a[i];
    normB += b[i] * b[i];
  }
  return dot / (Math.sqrt(normA) * Math.sqrt(normB));
}
```

## Enhanced Bullet Scoring
```typescript
async function scoreBulletsEnhanced(
  bullets: PlaybookBullet[],
  task: string,
  config: Config
): Promise<ScoredBullet[]> {
  // 1. Keyword scoring (fast, always run)
  const keywords = extractKeywords(task);
  const keywordScored = bullets.map(b => ({
    bullet: b,
    keywordScore: scoreBulletRelevance(b.content, b.tags, keywords)
  }));
  
  // 2. Semantic scoring (if enabled)
  if (!config.semanticSearchEnabled) {
    return keywordScored.map(({ bullet, keywordScore }) => ({
      ...bullet,
      combinedScore: keywordScore,
      effectiveScore: getEffectiveScore(bullet, config)
    }));
  }
  
  const taskEmbedding = await embedText(task);
  
  const semanticScored = await Promise.all(
    keywordScored.map(async ({ bullet, keywordScore }) => {
      // Load cached or compute embedding
      const bulletEmbedding = bullet.embedding || await embedText(bullet.content);
      const semanticScore = cosineSimilarity(taskEmbedding, bulletEmbedding);
      
      return {
        ...bullet,
        keywordScore,
        semanticScore,
        // Combined: 40% keyword + 60% semantic
        combinedScore: (keywordScore * 0.4) + (semanticScore * 10 * 0.6),
        effectiveScore: getEffectiveScore(bullet, config)
      };
    })
  );
  
  return semanticScored;
}
```

## Embedding Cache
```typescript
// ~/.cass-memory/embeddings/bullets.json
interface EmbeddingCache {
  version: string;
  model: string;
  bullets: Record<string, {
    contentHash: string;
    embedding: number[];
    computedAt: string;
  }>;
}

async function loadOrComputeEmbeddings(
  playbook: Playbook,
  cache: EmbeddingCache
): Promise<void> {
  for (const bullet of playbook.bullets) {
    const hash = hashContent(bullet.content);
    const cached = cache.bullets[bullet.id];
    
    if (cached && cached.contentHash === hash) {
      bullet.embedding = cached.embedding;
    } else {
      bullet.embedding = await embedText(bullet.content);
      cache.bullets[bullet.id] = {
        contentHash: hash,
        embedding: bullet.embedding,
        computedAt: now()
      };
    }
  }
  await saveEmbeddingCache(cache);
}
```

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-y9k","title":"[Foundation] tsconfig.json with strict TypeScript settings","description":"Create tsconfig.json with strict TypeScript configuration for type safety and ESM module support.

## Context
TypeScript configuration is critical for ensuring type safety, proper module resolution, and IDE support throughout the CASS Memory System codebase. The configuration must support ESM, strict type checking, and clean import paths.

## Technical Specification

### Compiler Options:
- target: ESNext - Use latest JavaScript features
- module: ESNext - ES module output
- moduleResolution: bundler - Modern resolution for Bun
- strict: true - Enable all strict type checking options
- esModuleInterop: true - Better compatibility with CommonJS modules
- skipLibCheck: true - Skip type checking of declaration files for faster builds
- resolveJsonModule: true - Allow importing JSON files
- allowSyntheticDefaultImports: true - Allow default imports from modules with no default export
- forceConsistentCasingInFileNames: true - Ensure consistent file name casing
- noImplicitAny: true - Disallow implicit any types
- strictNullChecks: true - Strict null checking
- strictFunctionTypes: true - Strict function type checking
- noUnusedLocals: true - Report errors on unused local variables
- noUnusedParameters: true - Report errors on unused parameters
- noImplicitReturns: true - Report error when not all code paths return a value
- noFallthroughCasesInSwitch: true - Report errors for fallthrough cases in switch

### Path Mapping:
- paths: { @/*: [./src/*] } - Clean imports like import { Playbook } from '@/types'

### Include/Exclude:
- include: [src/**/*] - Include all source files
- exclude: [node_modules, dist] - Exclude dependencies and build output

### Output:
- outDir: ./dist - Output directory for compiled files
- declaration: true - Generate .d.ts files for library use
- declarationMap: true - Generate sourcemaps for declarations
- sourceMap: true - Generate sourcemaps for debugging

## Reasoning

### Why Strict Mode:
1. Catch Bugs Early: Strict null checks prevent null/undefined errors at runtime
2. Better Refactoring: Type errors guide you when making changes
3. IDE Support: Better autocomplete and IntelliSense
4. LLM Integration: Type safety is crucial when parsing LLM responses

### Why ESNext Target:
1. Bun Support: Bun natively supports modern JavaScript
2. Smaller Polyfills: No need to transpile to ES5
3. Modern Features: Use async/await, optional chaining, etc.

### Why Bundler Module Resolution:
1. Modern Standard: Aligns with modern bundlers and runtimes
2. Better Imports: Supports package.json exports field
3. Bun Compatibility: Matches Bun's module resolution algorithm

### Why Path Mapping:
1. Clean Imports: @/types instead of ../../../types
2. Refactor-Friendly: Moving files doesn't break imports
3. Readability: Clear namespace separation

## Acceptance Criteria

- tsconfig.json exists in project root
- strict: true is enabled
- target: ESNext is set
- module: ESNext is set
- moduleResolution: bundler is set
- Path mapping configured for @/* -> ./src/*
- include/exclude properly configured
- outDir set to ./dist
- bun run typecheck runs without errors on initial project
- IDE shows proper IntelliSense for imports
- No implicit any warnings in editor

## How This Serves the Universal Agent Memory System

TypeScript configuration is the quality gate for the entire codebase:

1. Type Safety: Prevents runtime errors when parsing LLM responses, file operations, and config management
2. Developer Experience: IntelliSense and autocomplete make development faster and more reliable
3. Maintainability: Strong types serve as living documentation for the codebase
4. Refactoring Confidence: Type errors guide safe refactoring as the system evolves
5. LLM Output Validation: Zod schemas combined with TypeScript types ensure structured outputs match expectations
